coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of N-doped carbon quantum dots modified SnO2 composites for improved visible light photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yuwei
Jiang, Yongfeng
Chen, Bingyan
Ye, Fanglong

coredata.dc:description: In this study, The N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) modified SnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized by the facile hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic performance of resultant SnO2/NCQDs nanoparticles were indeed investigated which indicated the SnO2/NCQDs exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance than the SnO2. The improved photocatalytic activity of the SnO2/NCQDs composites could be due to the introduction of NCQDs, which can not only increase visible light absorption spectrum, but also improve the separation efficiency of the electrons and holes leaded to the accelerated electron transfer.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, The N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) modified SnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized by the facile hydrothermal method. The photocatalytic performance of resultant SnO2/NCQDs nanoparticles were indeed investigated which indicated the SnO2/NCQDs exhibited enhanced photocatalytic performance than the SnO2. The improved photocatalytic activity of the SnO2/NCQDs composites could be due to the introduction of NCQDs, which can not only increase visible light absorption spectrum, but also improve the separation efficiency of the electrons and holes leaded to the accelerated electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of industrialization, Water pollution is becoming more and more serious. As world health organization (WHO) reporting, the more than 99% of the world's water resources cannot be safely consumed by human beings [1,2]. As the main sources of pollution, organic dyes which are partially toxic and harmful to human health were extensively utilized in the textile, paper manufacture, printing, food processing industries and so on [3]. Due to the stable chemical structure of the dyes, most of these compounds cannot be effectively removed by conventional methods such as coagulation, sedimentation, filtration, absorption and membrane technologies [4]. Therefore, it is highly imperative to develop well-defined technology for aqueous dye degradation from the point of global scarcity of potable water.

Advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), as the effective methods for decomposition organic wastewater, have received more and more attention in recent years [5,6]. Various AOPs have been used for the decontamination wastewater, including photocatalysis [7,8], ozonation [9,10], Fenton oxidation [11,12], non-thermal plasma [13,14] and their synergistic effects [15,16]. Among these methods, photocatalytic degradation is one of the most fascinating techniques owing to its simplicity, superior removal efficiency and no secondary pollution. During the past decades, many kinds of nanostructured materials (e.g. TiO2 [17,18], ZnO [19–21], g-C3N4 [22,23] and WO3 [24,25]) have been synthesized and used as photocatalytic materials to eliminate the pollutants. Given the limited industrial application, several studies haven designed and explored new photocatalysts with superior surface area, morphology and functionality [26,27].

Tin oxide (SnO2), as a versatile semiconductor, has attracted a great deal of attention as a photoactive material duo to its excellent optical properties such as high transparency, chemical and thermal stability, and wide band gap energy [28]. Because of these properties, SnO2 has been widespread applications in the fields of gas sensors, solar cells, batteries, electrochemical sensing, and many others [29–31]. The material also has strong oxidation performance and resistant to photo-erosion, making it an ideal photocatalytic material [32,33]. However, few applications of SnO2 have been developed for the sewage treatment due to the wide band gap energy and the rapid recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. SnO2 just responds to ultraviolet light, which is only 3–5% in the solar spectrum [34]. The utilization of visible light is extremely low. At the same time, a large amount of UV lamps using for removal pollutant will increase costs [35]. In order to extend the absorption range of SnO2, various methods have been adopted, including integrating with a narrow band-gap semiconductor, adjusting the surface oxygen vacancy, doping with metal ion and so on [36,37]. Asaithambi et al. [38]. synthesized alkaline earth metal doping SnO2 nanoparticles, which were used to decomposed methylene blue under visible light irradiation. Compared with the bare SnO2, the Mg-doped SnO2 catalyst has a higher photodegradation efficiency. The efficient photocatalytic performance was attributed to the recombination of electron-hole pairs was reduced and oxygen vacancies forming on the surface of SnO2. Tuan et al. [39] using reduced graphene oxide modified SnO2 to degradation methylene blue. The improved photocatalytic activity can be attributed to the raised specific surface area.

N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), as a new member of nanocarbon family, have been used in photocatalytic duo to their unique properties [40,41]. NCQDs improved photocatalytic performances by improving electro-hole separation and increasing the photo-absorption region [42,43]. According to previous work, the NCQDs could effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 [44,45], BiPO4 [46], and g-C3N4 [47,48] for breaking down organic pollutants. Thus, we are inspired to construct the NCQDs modified SnO2 photocatalyst to achieve visible-light photocatalytic activity for pollutant degradation.

In this work, SnO2 with NCQDs composites were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method. The crystal structures and physical properties of the composites were inquired in detail. The photocatalytic performances of the prepared composites on the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) were evaluated under simulated sunlight irradiation. Based on the reactive species trapping experiments and the transient photocurrent response, the probable degradation mechanisms were proposed.

All of the chemical reagents were of analytical grade purity without further purification, which were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (China). Deionized (DI) water was applied throughout this study.

The NCQDs aqueous dispersion were synthesized according to a previously reported method with minor modifications [49]. In general, a 20 mmol of citrate acid and 1.5 mL ethylenediamine were mixed with 50 mL DI. The solution was mixed for 30 min to obtain transparent solution and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (TLSA) at 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down, the obtained product was subjected to dialysis for 12 h. According to gravimetric analysis, the concentration of NCQDs was calibrated to be about 10 mg mL−1.

The SnO2/NCQDs were synthesized by a facile hydrothermal approach. 2 mmol of SnCl4⋅5H2O was added into 40 mL DI and stirred 30 min. Then, different amount of NCQDs aqueous was added, and the solution became to brown immediately. After magnetically stirring for 2 h, ammonia was added dropwise to the above solution and the pH was maintained at 9.0–10.0. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a 100 mL TLSA at 180 °C for 16 h. Finally, the products were centrifugalized and washed three times with DI and ethanol respectively and vacuum dried at 50 °C for 24 h. The loading amount of NCQDs were 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 wt %, which were signed as SD-0.5, SD-1.0, and SD-1.5, respectively. By comparison, pure SnO2 was synthesized by the same route without adding NCQDs.

The crystal structures of the synthesized materials were investigated on a D8-advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker) using Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.154178 nm). Field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Sigma 500, ZEISS) was used to characterize the morphology of the photocatalyst under an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. TEM was performed using a FEI TalosF200x (Thermo Fisher) microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was achieved using an Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250 spectrometers with a monochromatic Al Ka ray source (1486.6 eV) at power of 150W (15 kV, 10 mA). The base pressure was about 2.5 × 10−9 mbar. The data were collected using 20 eV pass energy and 0.15 dwelling time. A charge neutralizer was applied to correct for sample charging. The C–C/C–H binding energy of adventitious carbon (AdC) is selected as the reference binding energy (BE). However, the position of AdC is often affected by the substrate, environment and exposure time, and the variation may be as high as 2.66 eV [50,51]. Greczynski and Hultman reported that the sum of the C–C/C–H peak BE of AdC and the work function (ϕ SA) of the sample is constant, and its value is 289.58 ± 0.14 eV [52]. Based on this information, the BE of C–C/C–H peak of Adc (ESD) can be calculated by the following expression [53].
(1) ESD = 289.58-ϕ SD

The work function of SD can be measured by UPS with a monochromatic He I light source (21.2 eV) and a spherical analyzer under PHI5000 Versa Probe III. The work function can be determined by the difference between the photon energy and the binding energy of the secondary cutoff edge (SEC).
(2) Work Function (ϕ SA) = 21.2- SEC

The SEC was shown as Fig. 1 , and its value was 16.25 eV. Therefore, the work function value is 4.95eV. The reference BE of the AdC for SD was 284.63 eV, which was set as the reference for calibration. The conclusion consistent with the previous reported [54,55]. Before testing, the samples were reduced at 350 °C for 4 h in pure hydrogen and sealed in a centrifuge tube. Netzsch STA 449F3 was used to obtain the Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) curves. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was tested on a PerkinElmer Lambda 950 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer. The optical band gap of the photocatalyst can be calculated by using the Kubelka-Munk equation [56]. FT-IR spectrum was recorded on Bruker Tensor 27 spectrometer with the photocatalyst dispersed in KBr in the range from 4000 to 400 cm−1. The transient photoluminescence (PL) decay spectra were carried out on a HORIBA Jobin Yvon FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer using 315 nm laser.

The photocatalytic performances of the catalyst were investigated by photodegradation of RhB under simulated solar light irradiation of a 300 W xenon lamp (CEL-HXF300, Beijing AuLight). In all experiment, 50 mg of catalyst was scattered in 50 mL of RhB solution. The initial concentration of RhB solutions was 5 mg L−1. Before photocatalytic reaction, the solutions were magnetically mixed in the dark for 30 min. During the photocatalytic process, 3 mL of the suspension was collected at given time intervals and centrifuged. The concentration of RhB solution was analyzed by UV–Vis–NIR PE Lambda 950 spectrophotometer at the strongest absorption peak. During the recycle test, the sample was separated by centrifuging and washing several times with DI and ethanol before new cycle photocatalytic. In addition, the free radical studies were conducted using different scavengers including tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) as hydroxyl radical, benzoquinone (BQ) as superoxide radical, and EDTA-2Na as holes radical. The formation of •OH radicals during photocatalytic process were examined via a terephthalic acid (TA) [57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modification of FTO for the working electrodes and photoelectrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: The photoelectrochemical property of the samples were measured on an electrochemical system (Squidstat Plus, Admiral). 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was utilized as electrolytes in this project. The working electrode was prepared according to a previous report [58]. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (M − S) plots were performed under the following working conditions: for EIS, frequency range = 1–105 Hz, for M − S, the conduction was at 1000 Hz.

Based on the catalyst degradation data in section 3.3, the catalyst with the highest RhB degradation efficiency was selected as the optimum catalyst. The addition of NCQDs to SnO2 catalytic led to a notable increase photocatalytic degradation of RhB, especially the best doping amount of 1.0 wt% NCQDs (SD-1.0). Accordingly, as the best-performed photocatalyst, the SD-1.0 was selected to compare with SnO2 for characterization and further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure of the catalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal phase of as-synthesized SD-1.0 composites and pure SnO2 were investigated by XRD. Fig. 2 revealed the diffraction peaks of as-samples locate at 26.6°, 33.9°, 37.9°, 51.8°, 54.8°, and 65.9° corresponding to the (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), and (301) crystal planes of tetragonal SnO2 (JCPDS NO. 41–1445), respectively [59]. The SD-1.0 composites showed SnO2 peaks with lower intensity than pure SnO2, while we observed no diffraction peaks for the NCQDs (2θ–26.0°) [60]. The NCQDs doping did not significantly change the crystal structure of SnO2, which was due to the low content of NCQDs doping in the composites [42]. The crystallite sizes of the synthesized photocatalytic were calculated using the Scherrer formula at the (110) crystal plane [61]. The crystallite sizes of the SnO2 and SD-1.0 were 2.6 nm and 4.3 nm, respectively. Therefore, the crystal grains of SnO2 were affected by the NCQDs doping and became smaller.

The chemical states and surface composition of the SnO2 and SD-1.0 photocatalytic were investigated using XPS, and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . The survey scan (Fig. 3a) confirmed that the existence elements contained Sn and O on the surface of both SnO2 and SD-1.0 composites. The high-resolution spectra of Sn3d, O1s, and C1s were depicted in Fig. 3b–d. The peak at 531.5 eV can be fitted to the lattice oxygen of SnO2, while that at 530.8 eV should be from the oxygen of the –OH group at the composite surface [61,62]. In addition, Fig. 3d exhibits that the high-resolution scanning XPS spectrum of the C1s could be split into four peaks at 288.8, 286.3, 285.0 and 284.6 eV, which correspond to CO, C–OH, C–N, and C–C, respectively [63]. The peak at 399.8 eV in the N1s spectra was attributed to graphitic N, which implied that N atom displaced the C element inside the carbon dots [64]. These results of XPS analysis implied the coexistence of SnO2 and NCQDs in the SD-1.0 photocatalytic.

The morphology of the SnO2/NCQDs composites were investigated by SEM analysis. As can be seen from Fig. 4 a, the presence of NCQDs can hardly be perceived from the SEM images of SnO2. This is possibly due to too little NCQDs doping in the composites. The enlarged morphology in the dotted area of Fig. 4a was shown in Fig. 4b. From the figure, it can be seen that the NCQDs with less than 10 nm size were wrapped on the surface of the SnO2.

The SnO2/NCQDs hybrid materials were analyzed by TEM and HR-TEM as depicted in Fig. 5 . The TEM image in Fig. 5a showed that the grain size of the SD-1.0 sample with sheet shaped structure was in the range of 5–8 nm (inset of Fig. 5a). As shown in Fig. 5b, the fringes with the interplanar spacing of ca. 0.221 and 0.341 nm were belonged to the lattice plane of NCQDs and SnO2 which attributed to the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon [65] and the (110) spacing of SnO2 (JPCDS 41–1445) [59], respectively. The EDS mapping was also carried out to investigate the crystallinity and composition of the samples. Fig. 5c shows that the SD-1.0 sample contained the Sn, O and C elements. Quantitative results showed the atomic ratio of 1:2.3 for Sn/O. Considering the instrumental error, this value was close to the ideal value of 2. EDX analysis was performed to confirm the elemental composition of SnO2 nanoparticles and NCQDs. Fig. 5d-g showed that Sn, O, C and N element were all existed in SD-1.0 composites and C element is evenly distributed in the composites. The above results confirmed the formation of the SnO2/NCQDs heterostructure, which was beneficial to photogenerated charge separation and electron transfer.

The TGA cures of SnO2 and SD-1.0 were shown in Fig. 6 . Compared with SnO2, the SD-1.0 has a more weight loss in the range of 100–600 °C, which mainly contributed to dihydroxylation of surface and the part combustion of NCQDs [66]. The results implied that the addition of NCQDs could improve the ability of SnO2 to adsorb hydroxyl groups on the surface.

The FTIR spectra of SnO2 and SD-1.0 samples were exhibited in Fig. 7 a. The strong signals of SnO2 in the range of 480–670 cm−1 were correspond to Sn–O stretching modes of Sn–O–Sn, which was consistent with the reported values [67]. The peaks observed at 1640 and 3420 cm−1 were attributed to various Sn–OH vibrational modes. The Sn–OH vibrating modes could be bound up with prominent defects such as oxygen vacancies in the SnO2 crystals [29]. After the introduction of NCQDs, the characteristic peaks of CO, C–H, CH2 and CC–H were observed at 1270, 1400, 2820 and 3130 cm−1, testified to the existence of NCQDs in the photocatalyst complex [66]. Although the trace amount of NCQDs has been added in SnO2 catalysts, the characteristic peaks of Sn–O and Sn–OH were still easily found in these samples, which was due to its stable crystal phase. It was also observed that the broad absorption below 1000 cm−1 became wider when compared to that of SnO2. This result was associated to a combination of Sn–O–Sn and Sn–O–C vibrations by many researchers and suggests that the coupling between SnO2 and NCQD is achieved by the formation of the Sn–O–C band [68].

The optical absorption behaviors of SnO2 and SnO2/NCQDs composites with different NCQDs amounts were examined. As shown in Fig. 7b, the absorption edge of SnO2 was at about 375 nm, while the SD-1.0 shows an absorption band at about 395 nm. It can be seen that NCQDs can enhance the absorption spectrum of the matrix material. Compared to the pure SnO2, the sample of SD-1.0 showed red-shift in the absorption edges. The band gap of SnO2 and SD-1.0 were also calculated by using the Kubelka-Munk equation [58]. Generally, the red shift of the photocatalytic absorption spectrum could be accompanied by the narrowing of the band gap. As demonstrated in the inset Fig. 6b, the band gaps of SnO2 and SD-1.0 were calculated to be 3.31 and 3.14 eV, which were consistent with the previous reported [59].

The M − S usually was used to determine the conduction band (CB) and valance band (VB) of the semiconductor [62]. From Fig. 7c, the positive slopes of curves confirmed that the SnO2 is an n-type semiconductor. The flat-band potentials of SnO2 and SD-1.0 were estimated to be −1.06 and −0.95 eV versus a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode, and they were implied to be −0.87 and −0.76 eV versus an NHE. At the same times, the CB position of n-type semiconductor is 0.1–0.3 eV higher than of the flat-band potential [69]. Therefore, the CB of SnO2 and SD-1.0 were conjectured to be −1.17 and −1.06 eV. Hence, the VB positions of SnO2 and SD-1.0 were calculated as 2.14 and 2.08 eV according to the empirical formula:
E
V B

E
C B

E g
.

The PL emission spectra was investigated to demonstrate the effective separation of electron and hole in the as-prepared sample [70]. PL analysis of SnO2, SD-0.5, SD-1.0 and SD-1.5 were exhibited in Fig. 7d. In the PL spectrum of SnO2 displayed a strong emission peak at 437 nm as compared to SD-1 composites, implying that it has fast recombination of e−-h+. After the loading of NCQDs, the PL intensity decreased notablely, implying that the recombination of electron and hole pairs have been hindered [71,72].

Degradation of RhB by the as-synthesized samples of SnO2 and SnO2/NCQDs materials were investigated under visible light radiation. The RhB degradation with and without the photocatalytic were shown in Fig. 8 a. It was observed that the synthesis of SnO2/NCQDs composites have synergic effects that are very advantageous for decomposing the RhB. After visible-light irradiation for 120 min, only 20.1% of RhB was degraded by SnO2. However, the NCQDs was embedded into the SnO2, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB was significantly increased. The SD-1.0 composites gained the maximum photocatalytic activity. The 99.0% degradation was achieved when using SD-1.0 composites as a photocatalyst after 120 min. Compared with the SnO2/NCQDs photocatalytic prepared by the hydrothermal method, the physical mixture of SnO2 and NCQDs (mix SnO2+NCQDs) has lower photocatalytic activity. The results showed that the hydrothermal method was beneficial for NCQDs to effectively adhere to the surface of SnO2, which could improve photocatalytic performance. Besides, the kinetic behavior of as-prepared samples for photodegradation of RhB was studied in Fig. 8b. The rate constant was 0.0522 min−1 for the SnO2 and 0.0902, 0.3932, 0.1854 and 0.0204 min−1 for SD-0.5, SD-1.0, SD-1.5, blank, respectively. The rate constant of SD-1.0 is 7.53 times higher than SnO2. This result also showed that the NCQDs is beneficial for photoactivity.

The image of Fig. 8c is the UV–vis absorption spectra images collected at different times of photocatalytic degradation RhB dye. It can be seen that the intensity of the characteristic peak of RhB at 554 nm was significantly reduced during the period of light radiation from 0 min to 120 min. During the degradation process, the absorption characteristic peak of RhB occurred a clear blue shift, which is consistent with the results observed by Fu et al. in the RhB/Bi2WO6 system [73]. Under visible irradiate, the maximum absorption peak of RhB, which was attributed to the chromophore's functional groups and its dimers, changing with the radiation time was taken. The degraded product underwent de-ethylated, and the color of the solution changes from red to green and yellow. During the fully N,N,N′,N'-tetraethylated rhodamine molecule is de-ethylation, its major absorption wavelength position shifts to the blue region, and the maximum absorption peak is reduced from 552 to 498 nm. After completely de-methylation, the conjugated structure will be destroyed and fully degraded [73,74].

In order to investigate the stability and reproducibility of the photocatalyst performance of SD-1.0, another two cycles of degradation RhB tests were examined under the same experimental conditions. The results were demonstrated in Fig. 8d, confirming that it was little change in the degradation rate after three cycles of photocatalytic processes. Thus, the as-synthesized SD-1.0 photocatalyst has excellent photocatalytic stability and reusability.

The separation of the photogenerated electron and hole pairs was investigated by the photocurrent curve [75,76]. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the SD-1.0 presents higher photocurrent than SnO2. The photocurrent density of SD-1.0 reached 0.82
μ A
cm−2 and the value were 3.56 times higher than the value of SnO2 (0.23
μ A
cm−2). The increased photocurrent implies that the generated charge-carriers in the SD-1.0 were separated much more efficiently than pure SnO2. Generally, the transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes positively correlated with the intensity of photocurrent [77]. Thus, SD-1.0 could produce much more electrons and holes to involve in the photodegradation process, acquiring the better photocatalytic activity. The EIS Nyquist plots of the SnO2 and SD-1.0 were demonstrated in Fig. 9b. Compared with SnO2, the arc radius of the SD-1.0 shows to be smaller, implying that SD-1.0 heterojunction has more transfer efficiency of electron and hole pairs driven by the internal electric field and interface [78,79].

In order to understand the roles of a series of active species on the degradation of RhB, the trapping experiments were implemented. Fig. 9c appeared that the degradation efficiency was slightly hindered after adding TBA (30 mM), indicating that the
·
OH
was not main dedicated to the reaction of the degradation. However, when the EDTA-2Na (5 mM) was added, the degradation efficiency was severely hindered, implying the h+ is the main reactive species in the SD-1.0 reactive system. BQ (2 mM) as a scavenger of
·
O 2 −
was employed into the degradation solution, the degradation efficiency was gigantic hampered. Therefore, it could be confirmed that
·
O 2 −
and h+ produced in photocatalytic system should be in charge of the improved photocatalytic activity towards RhB degradation under visible light irradiation.

The TA probe was used to capture the formed •OH radicals [80]. As showing in Fig. 9d, the maximum intensity at 425 nm gradually increased against the irradiation time, evidencing •OH photogeneration. The valence band position of SD-1.0 is at 2.08 V, which is not capable of indirect generation of •OH radicals by direct hole oxidation (
E 0

/ ·

)
= 2.4 V
) [81]. Therefore, the •OH were mainly converted from
·
O 2 −
radicals. This indirectly confirms that NCQDs can improve the electron transfer from CB to the surface of O2, hinder electron-hole recombination, and enhance the photocatalytic activity.

According to the above analysis results, Fig. 10 proposed a possible photocatalytic mechanism of the NCQDs-doped SnO2 composites. The degradation was acquired when visible light falls upon SnO2 nanoparticles produces in electron (e−) excitation from VB to the CB, creating holes in the VB. Due to the high carrier recombination rate of SnO2, a most portion of created electron-hole pairs recombined and thus showed less photocatalytic performance. The NCQDs-doped SnO2 composites showed enhanced photocatalytic performance due to have synergistic effect in reducing the recombining deficiency of created charges. The electron and hole on SnO2 surface were separated, and electron transfers to the CB and migrate to NCQDs, while hole remained in the VB, and the oxidizes contaminants. The e− reacted with oxygen molecule and resulted in the generation of superoxide anion (
·
O 2 −
). Meanwhile, the photocatalytic degradation of RhB in VB may be corresponded to the direct reaction with hole rather than
·
OH
. The possible reaction steps are summarized in the following equations.

products
(8)
RhB + ·
OH → degradable
products
At the same time, the band gap of SnO2 photocatalytic was reduced due to the doped NCQDs, which could increase light absorption range of the catalyst.

In short, it can draw a conclusion that the role of the NCQDs in the SD-1 composites could improve the separation and transfer of electron-hole pairs by photogenerated as well as increase light absorption for efficient degradation of pollutants.

The photocatalyst of SnO2 decorated with NCQDs was synthesized using a facile hydrothermal method. The SnO2/NCQDs composites showed enhanced photocatalytic performance in the degradation of RhB, which increased from 20.1% to 99.0% under 120 min visible irradiation. This enhanced photocatalytic activity might be assigned to NCQDs, which can interact with SnO2 to extending the response of the composites in visible light by narrowing the band gap. The NCQDs can also attribute to efficient charge separation and transporters, slowing down the recombination rate. An analysis of reactive species demonstrated that
·
O 2 −
and h+ played key roles during the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. Hence, our results demonstrated that the SnO2/NCQDs composites is a promising photocatalyst, which could be used to break down organic pollutants under visible light.
